section: Chapter 3: Electricity – Circuits and Their Components

questions.question: What is electric current?
questions.correct_answer: The flow of electric charge through a conductor
questions.explanation: Current flows from the positive to the negative terminal in the conventional direction.

questions.question: What is voltage?
questions.correct_answer: The potential difference between two points in a circuit
questions.explanation: Voltage provides the energy required to move charges through a conductor.

questions.question: What is resistance?
questions.correct_answer: The opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of electric current
questions.explanation: Resistance depends on the material, length, and thickness of the conductor.

questions.question: What is a series circuit?
questions.correct_answer: A circuit in which components are connected one after another
questions.explanation: Current is the same through all components, but voltage divides.

questions.question: What is a parallel circuit?
questions.correct_answer: A circuit in which components are connected across common points
questions.explanation: Voltage is the same across all components, but current divides.

questions.question: Name the main components of a simple electric circuit.
questions.explanation: Each component has a specific role in providing, conducting, converting, and controlling electrical energy.

questions.question: What is a short circuit?
questions.correct_answer: When current bypasses the intended path, causing a sudden surge
questions.explanation: Short circuits can damage components and cause fire, prevented using fuses or circuit breakers.

questions.question: How can electrical accidents be prevented?
questions.correct_answer: By using fuses, circuit breakers, insulated wires, and switches properly
questions.explanation: Avoid touching live wires or water while handling electricity to stay safe.

questions.question: State Ohm's Law.
questions.correct_answer: Current through a conductor is directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance (I = V/R)
questions.explanation: Ohm’s Law helps calculate current, voltage, or resistance in a circuit.

questions.question: Give an example of Ohm’s Law in action.
questions.correct_answer: A 10 Ω resistor connected to a 5 V battery has a current of 0.5 A

notes.title: Electric Current
notes.content: Flow of charge measured in amperes, from positive to negative in conventional direction.

notes.title: Voltage
notes.content: Potential difference measured in volts, provides energy to move charges.

notes.title: Resistance
notes.content: Opposition to current flow, depends on material, length, thickness; V = IR.

notes.title: Circuit Types
notes.content: Series: same current, voltage divides. Parallel: same voltage, current divides.

notes.title: Short Circuit
notes.content: Bypassing intended path causing current surge; prevented by fuses/circuit breakers.

facts.fact: Current is measured in amperes using an ammeter.
Voltage is the energy per charge, measured in volts.
Ohm's Law relates voltage, current, and resistance: V = IR.
Series circuits fail completely if one component breaks; parallel circuits continue to work.
Fuses and circuit breakers prevent excessive current from damaging circuits.
